cells.title: Overview
cells.source: This notebook adapts the `gl8-growth.Rmd` article into executable browser cells. It includes the full GROWTH baseline, the balance-sheet and transactions-flow matrices, and three representative policy experiments from the article: a higher target real wage, a one-period fiscal-growth shock, and a rise in non-performing loans.
cells.more:
Godley and Lavoie's Chapter 11 GROWTH model is the large stock-flow consistent growth model in _Monetary Economics_. It combines production, inventories, fixed capital, wage bargaining, household portfolio choice, government debt, central-bank accommodation, and commercial-bank balance sheets.

The implementation here follows the local `gl8-growth.Rmd` reference, which notes that this model is especially sensitive to exact equations and initial values. The browser version keeps the same baseline calibration and validates the model against the R regression fixture for selected baseline and scenario checkpoints.

cells.title: Chapter 11 reading guide
cells.source:
Chapter 11 turns the earlier SFC accounting framework into a growing monetary economy with production, bank credit, asset prices, and fiscal policy.

Read the notebook in three passes: first check the accounting matrices, then inspect the growth and finance equations, then run the scenarios and compare the ratios rather than only the levels.
cells.more:
The key Chapter 11 idea is that "growth" is not a single exogenous trend. Productivity grows at `GRpr`, government spending grows at `GRg`, capital grows through `gr`, and nominal balance sheets grow through the interaction of prices, quantities, lending, retained earnings, and portfolio choices.

The baseline is therefore best read through stabilizing ratios: utilization `U`, inflation `PI`, capital growth `gr`, bank liquidity `BLR`, capital adequacy `CAR`, government debt `GD`, and the balance-sheet residuals. Because most nominal stocks grow over time, raw levels can look explosive even when the model has settled into a balanced-growth path.

cells.title: GROWTH balance sheet
cells.description: Balance-sheet matrix for model GROWTH following the gl8 article.
cells.note: Validated against the baseline simulation in the R vignette fixture.
cells.more:
This matrix shows the Chapter 11 stock positions at a point in time. Firms hold inventories `IN` and fixed capital `K`; households hold money, bills, bonds, equities, and bank capital while owing household loans `L_hd`; banks issue deposits `Ms`, hold loans and bills, and carry own funds `OF_b`.

The final `Sum` row is the accounting check. If the model has solved consistently, every financial asset has a matching liability and the nonfinancial assets `IN + K` are matched by sector net worth.

cells.title: GROWTH transactions-flow matrix
cells.description: Transactions-flow matrix for model GROWTH following the gl8 article.
cells.note: Validated against the baseline simulation in the R vignette fixture.
cells.more:
The transactions-flow matrix connects production, income, finance, and changes in stocks. Notice the separate current and capital accounts for firms, the current and capital accounts for the central bank, and the current and capital accounts for banks.

The loan-default row is important for Chapter 11. `NPL` is a source to firms' capital account and a loss to banks' capital account, so a credit-quality shock changes both firms' financing need and banks' own funds without breaking stock-flow consistency.

cells.title: GROWTH transaction flow sequence
cells.description: Sequence view generated from the GROWTH transactions-flow matrix at the selected simulation period.

cells.title: GROWTH model

equations.name: IN
equations.desc: Stock of inventories at current costs

equations.name: omegat
equations.expression: exp(omega0 + omega1 * log(PR) + omega2 * log(ER + z3 * (1 - ER) - z4 * BANDt + z5 * BANDb))

equations.name: phit
equations.expression: (FU_f^T + FD_f + r_l[-1] * (L_fd[-1] - IN[-1])) / ((1 - sigmas^e) * s^e * UC + (1 + r_l[-1]) * sigmas^e * s^e * UC[-1])

equations.name: HCe
equations.expression: (1 - sigmas^e) * s^e * UC + (1 + r_l[-1]) * sigmas^e * s^e * UC[-1]

equations.name: sigmas^e
equations.desc: Opening inventories to expected sales ratio

equations.name: L_fd
equations.expression: L_fd[-1] + (INV + d(IN) - FU_f - d(e_s) * Pe - NPL)*dt

equations.name: e_s
equations.desc: Number of equities supplied by firms

equations.name: GL
equations.desc: Gross amount of new personal loans

equations.name: eta
equations.desc: Ratio of new loans to personal income

equations.name: NL
equations.desc: Net flow of new loans to the household sector

equations.name: nl
equations.desc: Real flow of new loans to the household sector

equations.name: B_hd
equations.desc: Demand for government bills from households
equations.expression: V_fma[-1] * (lambda20 + lambda22 * R_b[-1] - lambda21 * r_m[-1] - lambda24 * Rk[-1] - lambda23 * R_bl[-1] - lambda25 * (YD_r / V))

equations.name: BL_d
equations.expression: V_fma[-1] * (lambda30 - lambda32 * R_b[-1] - lambda31 * r_m[-1] - lambda34 * Rk[-1] + lambda33 * R_bl[-1] - lambda35 * (YD_r / V)) / P_bl

equations.name: Pe
equations.expression: V_fma[-1] * (lambda40 - lambda42 * R_b[-1] - lambda41 * r_m[-1] + lambda44 * Rk[-1] - lambda43 * R_bl[-1] - lambda45 * (YD_r / V)) / e_d

equations.name: M_h
equations.expression: V_fma - B_hd - Pe * e_d - P_bl * BL_d + L_hd

equations.name: VfmaA
equations.expression: M_h + B_hd + P_bl * BL_d + Pe * e_d

equations.name: B_cbd
equations.desc: Government bills demanded by Central bank

equations.name: B_cbs
equations.desc: Government bills supplied by Central bank

equations.name: B_bs
equations.desc: Government bills supplied to commercial banks

equations.name: B_bd
equations.desc: Government bills demanded by commercial banks

equations.name: r_m
equations.expression: r_m[-1] + z1a * xim1 + z1b * xim2 - z2a * xim1 - z2b * xim2

equations.name: z2a
equations.desc: Is one if bank liquidity ratio is above top range

equations.name: z2b
equations.desc: Is one if bank liquidity ratio is above top range

equations.name: z1a
equations.desc: Is one if bank liquidity ratio is below bottom range

equations.name: z1b
equations.desc: Is one if bank liquidity ratio is below bottom range

equations.name: OFb^T
equations.desc: Long-run target for banks own funds

equations.name: OFb^e
equations.desc: Short-run target for banks own funds

equations.name: F_b
equations.expression: r_l' * (L_fs' + L_hs' - NPL) + R_b' * B_bd' - r_m' * Ms'

equations.name: ADDl
equations.desc: Spread between interest rate on loans and rate on deposits

cells.more:
The equations follow the Chapter 11 blocks: production and inventories, wage and price setting, firm finance, household income and portfolio choice, government debt issuance, central-bank accommodation, and commercial-bank pricing and capital.

A few translation choices mirror the reference implementation rather than the printed textbook alone. The local R article explicitly says that the model only simulated reliably with Zezza-style equations and exact initial values, so the browser template preserves that calibration and then checks selected results against the R fixture.

cells.title: Solver
cells.more:
Chapter 11's GROWTH model has a dense simultaneous system. The hidden equation checks the banks' bills position, comparing `B_bs` with `B_bd`.

In the R reference, the absolute discrepancy grows with the scale of the economy, so the check is relative rather than absolute. This notebook uses the same idea through `relativeHiddenTolerance: true` and a hidden tolerance of `0.000001`.

externals.name: alpha1
externals.desc: Propensity to consume out of income

externals.name: alpha2
externals.desc: Propensity to consume out of wealth

externals.name: beta
externals.desc: Parameter in expectation formations on real sales

externals.name: betab
externals.desc: Spped of adjustment of banks own funds

externals.name: gamma
externals.desc: Speed of adjustment of inventories to the target level

externals.name: gamma0
externals.desc: Exogenous growth_mod in the real stock of capital

externals.name: gammar
externals.desc: Relation between the real interest rate and growth_mod in the stock of capital

externals.name: gammau
externals.desc: Relation between the utilization rate and growth_mod in the stock of capital

externals.name: delta
externals.desc: Rate of depreciation of fixed capital

externals.name: deltarep
externals.desc: Ratio of personal loans repayments to stock of loans

externals.name: eps
externals.desc: Parameter in expectation formations on real disposable income

externals.name: eps2
externals.desc: Speed of adjustment of mark-up

externals.name: epsb
externals.desc: Speed of adjustment in expected proportion of non-performing loans

externals.name: epsrb
externals.desc: Speed of adjustment in the real interest rate on bills

externals.name: eta0
externals.desc: Ratio of new loans to personal income - exogenous component

externals.name: etan
externals.desc: Speed of adjustment of actual employment to desired employment

externals.name: etar
externals.desc: Relation between the ratio of new loans to personal income and the interest rate on loans

externals.name: theta
externals.desc: Income tax rate

externals.name: lambda20
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bills

externals.name: lambda21
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bills

externals.name: lambda22
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bills

externals.name: lambda23
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bills

externals.name: lambda24
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bills

externals.name: lambda25
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bills

externals.name: lambda30
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bonds

externals.name: lambda31
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bonds

externals.name: lambda32
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bonds

externals.name: lambda33
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bonds

externals.name: lambda34
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bonds

externals.name: lambda35
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for bonds

externals.name: lambda40
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for equities

externals.name: lambda41
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for equities

externals.name: lambda42
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for equities

externals.name: lambda43
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for equities

externals.name: lambda44
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for equities

externals.name: lambda45
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for equities

externals.name: lambdab
externals.desc: Parameter determining dividends of banks

externals.name: lambdac
externals.desc: Parameter in households demand for cash

externals.name: xim1
externals.desc: Parameter in the equation for setting interest rate on deposits

externals.name: xim2
externals.desc: Parameter in the equation for setting interest rate on deposits

externals.name: ro
externals.desc: Reserve requirement parameter

externals.name: sigman
externals.desc: Parameter of influencing normal historic unit costs

externals.name: sigmat
externals.desc: Target inventories to sales ratio

externals.name: psid
externals.desc: Ratio of dividends to gross profits

externals.name: psiu
externals.desc: Ratio of retained earnings to investments

externals.name: omega0
externals.desc: Parameter influencing the target real wage for workers

externals.name: omega1
externals.desc: Parameter influencing the target real wage for workers

externals.name: omega2
externals.desc: Parameter influencing the target real wage for workers

externals.name: omega3
externals.desc: Speed of adjustment of wages to target value

externals.name: ADDbl
externals.desc: Spread between long-term interest rate and rate on bills

externals.name: BANDt
externals.desc: Upper range of the flat Phillips curve

externals.name: BANDb
externals.desc: Lower range of the flat Phillips curve

externals.name: bot
externals.desc: Bottom value for bank net liquidity ratio

externals.name: GRg
externals.desc: growth_mod of real government expenditures

externals.name: GRpr
externals.desc: growth_mod rate of productivity

externals.name: Nfe
externals.desc: Full employment level

externals.name: NCAR
externals.desc: Normal capital adequacy ratio of banks

externals.name: npl
externals.desc: Proportion of Non-Performing loans

externals.name: Rbbar
externals.desc: Interest rate on bills, set exogenously

externals.name: Rln
externals.desc: Normal interest rate on loans

externals.name: RA
externals.desc: Random shock to expectations on real sales

externals.name: top
externals.desc: Top value for bank net liquidity ratio

cells.title: Baseline run
cells.description: Baseline GROWTH simulation with the relative hidden-equation condition B_bs = B_bd.
cells.more:
The baseline is a balanced-growth reference path. By period 350, the R fixture checkpoints show `gr` close to `0.03`, matching productivity and government-expenditure growth, while `PI` is about `0.00699`.

Use this run as the counterfactual for the scenarios below. Scenario runs start from the baseline state and then apply a Chapter 11 shock over the listed periods.

cells.title: Baseline steady-state checks
cells.more:
This chart focuses on ratios and rates instead of nominal levels. The baseline checkpoint at period 350 has `gr = 0.029999995893`, `BLR = 0.061789629176`, and `CAR = 0.091491631401` in the R regression fixture.

That is the practical steady-state check: the growing economy is not stationary in levels, but the central ratios have become stable.

cells.title: Baseline variable summary
cells.more: The table gives the same baseline from a level-and-ratio perspective. Output `y`, consumption `c`, and investment `i` grow with the model, while `P`, `r_l`, `BLR`, and `CAR` help diagnose inflation, lending conditions, liquidity, and bank capital.

cells.title: Scenario 1
cells.source: Scenario 1 follows the article's first experiment: `omega0` rises from `-0.20594` to `-0.1` from periods 5 to 150, raising the target real wage.
cells.more:
In Chapter 11, wage aspiration enters through `omegat`, which depends on productivity `PR`, the employment rate `ER`, and the wage-bargaining switch variables around the employment band.

Raising `omega0` is an autonomous upward shift in the target real wage. The immediate channel is higher wage pressure, then unit costs and prices, then consumption, output, and financial ratios through the rest of the SFC system.

cells.title: Scenario 1: higher target real wage
cells.more:
This scenario starts from baseline period 100 and applies the wage-aspiration shock through period 150. The R fixture checkpoints show inflation rising to `PI = 0.011977896953` at period 10 and `PI = 0.021530213648` at period 150.

The bank ratios move too: `CAR` is `0.092908302016` at period 10 and `0.088460918334` at period 150, while `BLR` recovers from `0.018759286216` to `0.110854870595`.

cells.title: Scenario 1 inflation, wage, and output, Figure 11.2A
cells.more: Compare `PI`, `W`, `y`, and `c` to see how a wage target shock propagates. The wage adjustment is not isolated: higher wages affect unit costs, prices, regular disposable income, consumption, and the investment path.

cells.title: Scenario 1 demand components, Figure 11.2B
cells.more: Figure 11.2B compares investment, consumption, and output after the wage-aspiration shock. The article plots these against the benchmark continuation; this chart overlays the baseline reference and keeps the three real quantities on separate axes.

cells.title: Scenario 1 checkpoint ratios
cells.more: These variables are the regression checkpoint variables for the wage shock. They are useful because they span the price channel (`PI`), the lending channel (`r_l`), and bank balance-sheet conditions (`CAR`, `BLR`).

cells.title: Scenario 1B
cells.source: Scenario 1B combines the higher target real wage with a bill-rate increase: `omega0` rises to `-0.1` and `Rbbar` rises to `0.055` from periods 5 to 150.
cells.more: The published article uses this second experiment to reproduce Figures 11.2C and 11.2D. The interest-rate shock is paired with the wage shock so the real bill rate and output response can be compared with the textbook discussion.

cells.title: Scenario 1B: wage target plus bill-rate increase
cells.more: This is the article's "Scenario 1, second experiment". The local R article notes that choosing the bill-rate path required trial and error, so the browser notebook keeps the published shock values rather than treating them as a policy rule.

cells.title: Scenario 1B real bill rate, Figure 11.2C
cells.more: The article's `Rrb` helper is `R_b - PI`: the nominal bill rate net of price inflation. This chart shows whether the combined wage and bill-rate shock leaves the real bill rate close to its starting value.

cells.title: Scenario 1B real output, Figure 11.2D
cells.more: Figure 11.2D compares real output under the combined wage and interest-rate experiment with the counterfactual path. The baseline reference trace provides that comparison in the notebook.

cells.title: Scenario 2
cells.source: Scenario 2 applies the one-period fiscal-growth shock from the article: `GRg` rises from `0.03` to `0.035` for periods 10 to 11.
cells.more:
This is a shock to the growth rate of real government expenditure `g`, not a one-time addition to the level of `G`. The higher `GRg` applies only briefly, but because `g = g[-1] * (1 + GRg)`, the level of government spending is left on a higher path.

Chapter 11 uses this type of experiment to show how demand, employment, debt, bank liquidity, and firm finance respond when fiscal growth temporarily outruns the baseline balanced-growth path.

cells.title: Scenario 2: one-period fiscal growth shock
cells.more:
The R fixture checkpoints for this shock show the short-run adjustment rather than a new calibration: at period 10, `BLR = 0.063644153424` and `CAR = 0.091640447259`; by period 25, `BLR = 0.079214382562` and `CAR = 0.091645964037`.

Track `PSBR` and `GD` with output because government debt is endogenous. It reflects spending, taxes, interest payments, bond issuance, bill issuance, and central-bank accommodation.

cells.title: Scenario 2 output and government spending, Figure 11.3A
cells.more: The `g` series shows the fiscal impulse itself. The response of `y` captures the demand side, while `PSBR` and `GD` show how the public-sector balance sheet absorbs the shock in the accounting system.

cells.title: Scenario 2 employment rate, Figure 11.3B
cells.more: The article adds guide lines around the employment band. This chart uses a narrow shared range around one so the employment-rate deviation is visible.

cells.title: Scenario 2 deficit and debt ratios, Figure 11.3C
cells.more: The article's `DefY` and `GDY` helpers are `PSBR / Y` and `GD / Y`. These ratios keep the fiscal response readable in a growing nominal economy.

cells.title: Scenario 2 firm loans, inventories, and bank liquidity, Figure 11.3D
cells.more: Figure 11.3D links the fiscal shock to firm finance and bank liquidity. The `LIN` helper in the article is `L_fd / IN`.

cells.title: Scenario 2 checkpoint ratios
cells.more: This checkpoint table pairs the price and lending-rate response with the banking ratios. It is a compact way to compare the fiscal-growth shock with the wage shock and the loan-default shock.

cells.title: Scenario 2B
cells.source: Scenario 2B applies the article's one-shot income-tax cut: `theta` falls from `0.22844` to `0.22` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: This experiment is the tax-side counterpart to the government-spending shock. It works first through regular disposable income, then consumption, output, taxes, and the public-sector balance sheet.

cells.title: Scenario 2B consumption and output, Figure 11.3E
cells.more: Figure 11.3E shows the demand response to a lower tax rate. The mechanism is direct: lower `theta` raises regular disposable income `YD_r`, which supports consumption `c` and output `y`.

cells.title: Scenario 3
cells.source: Scenario 3 makes the fiscal-growth shock permanent: `GRg` rises from `0.03` to `0.035` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: Unlike Scenario 2, the higher growth rate of real government spending is sustained. This lets the notebook reproduce Figures 11.4A through 11.4C.

cells.title: Scenario 3 growth rates, Figure 11.4B
cells.more: The article's `GRy` helper is `y / y' - 1`, the period growth rate of real output.

cells.title: Scenario 4
cells.source: Scenario 4 applies the article's staged permanent increase in the bill rate: `Rbbar` rises to `0.038`, then `0.041`, then `0.044`, then `0.047`.
cells.more: The staged path avoids a single large jump in interest rates. It exposes the household-debt, government-debt, and bank-rate channels in Figures 11.5A through 11.5E.

cells.title: Scenario 4 household loans to income, Figure 11.5D

series.label: Household loans to regular disposable income

cells.title: Scenario 5
cells.source: Scenario 5 increases the propensity to consume out of regular income: `alpha1` rises from `0.75` to `0.80` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: This demand shock is the source for Figures 11.7A through 11.7G. It changes consumption directly and then works through prices, retained earnings, inventories, fiscal balances, and equity valuation.

cells.title: Scenario 5: higher income propensity to consume
Scenario 5 normal costs and mark-up, Figure 11.7D
Scenario 5 retained earnings and inventories, Figure 11.7E
Scenario 5 Tobin's Q and price-earnings ratio, Figure 11.7G
Scenario 7
cells.source: Scenario 7 increases the gross new loans to personal income ratio by raising `eta0` from `0.07416` to `0.08416` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: This is the household-credit experiment behind Figures 11.8A through 11.8E. The first-order effect is more gross borrowing; the model then traces consumption, bank liquidity, loan pricing, and fiscal feedbacks.

cells.title: Scenario 7 household leverage and burden, Figure 11.8A

series.label: Household loans to regular disposable income

cells.title: Scenario 7 bank liquidity and capital, Figure 11.8C
Scenario 8
cells.source: Scenario 8 raises the household desire to hold equities: `lambda40` rises from `0.67132` to `0.77132` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: The article notes that changing one portfolio parameter is offset by the hidden deposit allocation. Figures 11.9A through 11.9C show the equity-price, activity, and bank-rate responses.

cells.title: Scenario 8: higher desire to hold equities
Scenario 8 real activity and wealth, Figure 11.9B
Scenario 8 loan and deposit rates, Figure 11.9C
Scenario 8B
cells.source: Scenario 8B offsets the higher equity preference with lower desires to hold bills and bonds: `lambda20` falls to `0.20` and `lambda30` falls to `-0.09341`.
cells.more: This scenario keeps the equity-preference shock from Scenario 8 and offsets it with lower bill and bond preferences. It backs Figure 11.9D.

cells.title: Scenario 9
cells.source: Scenario 9 raises the target proportion of gross investment financed by retained earnings: `psiu` rises from `0.92` to `1` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: This changes firms' desired internal finance and drives Figures 11.10A through 11.10E.

cells.title: Scenario 9 Tobin's Q and price-earnings ratio, Figure 11.10D
Scenario 9 real profit and equity-price growth, Figure 11.10E
Scenario 10
cells.source: Scenario 10 raises non-performing loans as in the article: `npl` rises from `0.02` to `0.05` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more:
The Chapter 11 banking block treats defaults as both a flow loss and an expectation problem. Actual non-performing loans are `NPL = npl * L_fs[-1]`, expected default intensity evolves through `npl^e`, and banks use that expectation when setting target retained earnings and loan spreads.

This makes the shock a balance-sheet stress experiment. It lowers bank own funds through `OF_b = OF_b[-1] + (FU_b - NPL) * dt`, changes the target profit calculation, and eventually feeds into the lending rate `r_l`.

cells.title: Scenario 10: higher non-performing loans
cells.more:
The R fixture shows the stress clearly. At period 10, `CAR = 0.078014236581`; by period 150, `r_l = 0.079990862443`, `BLR = 0.102248956781`, and `PI = -0.000108494375`.

The shock is temporary, but expectations and accumulated bank capital make the response persistent. Use this run to inspect how the commercial-bank block transmits credit losses into interest rates and real activity.

cells.title: Scenario 10 capital adequacy, Figure 11.11A
cells.more: The chart places the direct default flow `NPL` beside bank capital adequacy `CAR`, the loan rate `r_l`, and bank own funds `OF_b`. This is the banking channel that distinguishes Chapter 11 from simpler growth models without commercial-bank capital.

cells.title: Scenario 10 deposit and loan rates, Figure 11.11B
cells.more: Figure 11.11B follows the interest-rate response to higher non-performing loans. Expected defaults raise banks' desired retained earnings and feed into the lending spread.

cells.title: Scenario 10 checkpoint ratios
cells.more: These are the same variables used in the R regression checkpoint for the non-performing-loan shock. They show bank solvency, bank pricing, liquidity, and the macro price response in one compact view.

cells.title: Scenario 10B
cells.source: Scenario 10B raises the normal capital adequacy ratio: `NCAR` rises from `0.1` to `0.11` from periods 10 to 150.
cells.more: This is the companion banking experiment in the article. It asks what happens when banks target a higher capital adequacy norm rather than facing a higher direct default rate.

cells.title: Scenario 10B normal and actual capital adequacy, Figure 11.11C
GROWTH equation dependency graph
cells.description: Dependency view of the GROWTH equations organized by sector strips and accounting bands.
cells.note: This view is intentionally dense. Use it to inspect how the large GROWTH model clusters into exogenous inputs, intermediate flows, and downstream stocks.
cells.more:
The dependency graph is dense because Chapter 11 is dense. Use it to find the feedback loops behind each scenario: wage targets feed prices and income, fiscal growth feeds output and debt, and loan defaults feed bank capital, loan pricing, and investment finance.

The graph is not a substitute for the matrices. The matrices prove the accounting; the dependency graph helps explain why the solved path moves the way it does.
